title: PDF attachments are corrupted after upload
body: When customers upload PDF files to their tasks, the files come through broken. You can't open them — the PDF viewer says the file is damaged. This is happening for multiple customers. We use the file attachment feature heavily so this is really impacting our workflow.

comments.author: triage-agent
comments.body:
Thanks for reporting this — corrupted file uploads are definitely something we want to get to the bottom of quickly.

A few things to start narrowing this down: **Did PDF uploads work correctly before, and if so, when did you first notice the corruption?** Also, is this happening with *every* PDF you upload, or only certain ones (e.g., large files, scanned documents, PDFs with specific features like forms or signatures)?

Finally, can you confirm: are the files corrupted immediately after upload (i.e., if you download the file right after uploading, it's already broken), or does the corruption seem to happen later?

comments.author: reporter
comments.body: It was working fine until maybe a week ago? I'm not sure exactly when it started but it's recent. And no, not every PDF — I've noticed some smaller ones seem to go through okay but honestly most of the ones our customers send are pretty big reports and those are all broken. They're corrupted right away, you download them and they're already messed up. Can you just fix this? Our support team is dealing with angry customers every day over this.

comments.author: triage-agent
comments.body:
I understand the urgency — your support team shouldn't have to deal with this, and we'll work to get it resolved.

The size correlation is a really useful clue. To help our developers zero in on this:

**Can you pick one of the corrupted PDFs and tell me (1) the file size of the original before upload, and (2) the file size of the downloaded (corrupted) copy?** Specifically, are they the same size, or is the downloaded version smaller (truncated) or larger?

This will tell us whether the file is being cut off during upload, or whether the data is being mangled in a different way (e.g., encoding issue).

comments.author: reporter
comments.body: I don't know the exact file sizes off the top of my head, I'd have to go check. But I'm pretty sure the corrupted ones are not smaller — if anything they might be a little different in size but I didn't really compare them side by side. The point is they're just broken, the PDF viewer says they're damaged. Can someone on your end just try uploading a big PDF and see for themselves? It's not hard to reproduce.

comments.author: triage-agent
comments.body:
Totally hear you — and yes, our developers should absolutely try to reproduce this on their end too. That's a great call.

One more thing that will really help them target the right code: **Are other file types also corrupted when uploaded (e.g., Word docs, images, spreadsheets), or is this strictly a PDF problem?** And roughly, what size are the PDFs that break — are we talking 5 MB, 20 MB, 100 MB+? A ballpark is fine.

This tells us whether the issue is in how we handle uploads generally or something specific to how we process PDFs.

comments.author: reporter
comments.body: I don't know about other file types honestly, I mainly deal with PDFs because that's what our customers send us. The broken ones are usually like 2-5 MB range I think? Look, I'm not your QA team — can someone just upload a few PDFs and see what happens? It should be obvious.

comments.author: triage-agent
comments.body: [RESOLVED] {"title":"PDF attachments corrupted on upload for files in ~2-5 MB range (regression, ~1 week)","problem":"PDF files uploaded to tasks are immediately corrupted — downloading them right after upload yields a damaged file that PDF viewers cannot open. The issue affects most customer-uploaded PDFs, which tend to be in the 2-5 MB range. Smaller PDFs appear to upload correctly. This is a regression that began approximately one week ago.","root_cause_hypothesis":"A recent change (likely deployed ~1 week ago) introduced a bug in the file upload path that affects files above a certain size threshold. Likely candidates: (1) a chunked/multipart upload boundary that kicks in at a size threshold is corrupting the reassembled file, (2) an encoding or content-type handling change (e.g., base64 encoding binary data, or incorrect Transfer-Encoding) that mangles larger payloads, or (3) a middleware or proxy change (e.g., request body size limit, compression, or streaming behavior) that truncates or transforms the upload stream. The size correlation strongly suggests the upload code path diverges based on file size.","reproduction_steps":["Obtain any PDF file in the 2-5 MB range (a multi-page report or scanned document works)","Create or open a task in TaskFlow","Upload the PDF using the file attachment feature","Immediately download the uploaded file","Attempt to open the downloaded PDF — it should be reported as damaged/corrupted","Repeat with a small PDF (~100 KB) to confirm small files are unaffected"],"environment":"Not browser- or OS-specific per the report (multiple customers affected). Server-side issue. Check deployment history for changes in the past ~7-10 days.","severity":"high","impact":"Multiple customers are affected daily. The support team is fielding complaints. The file attachment feature is core to the reporter's workflow with their customers. No known workaround exists besides avoiding PDF uploads.","recommended_fix":"1. Review all deployments and code changes from the past 7-10 days touching: file upload endpoints, multipart handling, storage service integration, middleware (body parsers, compression, proxies), and any infrastructure changes (CDN, load balancer, object storage config). 2. Reproduce locally by uploading a 2-5 MB PDF. 3. Compare the byte content of the original file with the stored/downloaded copy — look for truncation, encoding artifacts (e.g., base64 wrapping of binary), or injection of extra bytes. 4. Check if there is a file-size threshold in the upload code where handling diverges (e.g., streaming vs. buffered, single-part vs. multipart). 5. Verify Content-Type headers are preserved as application/octet-stream or application/pdf through the full upload pipeline.","proposed_test_case":"Upload PDFs of varying sizes (100 KB, 1 MB, 2 MB, 5 MB, 10 MB) and verify byte-for-byte integrity by comparing SHA-256 checksums of the original and downloaded files. The test should fail if any checksum differs. Add this as an integration test against the upload endpoint.","information_gaps":["Whether non-PDF file types are also affected (reporter only uses PDFs — developers should test other types during reproduction)","Exact size threshold where corruption begins","Whether the stored file on the server/object storage is already corrupted or if corruption happens during download","Exact byte-level nature of the corruption (truncation vs. encoding vs. data transformation)"]}
